text: Hi everyone. Today's video is about an obese patient with a large abdominal wall incisional hernia.
She had a previous midline laparotomy. The patient has been told to lose weight before such elective surgery could be offered to her.

Whilst waiting, she presented to the emergency department with an obstructed abdominal wall incisional hernia. This was a large CT proven obstructed abdominal wall hernia with a main bulk on the left side of the midline.
The surgeon inserts the first Visiport blunt entry into the right upper quadrant of the patient's abdomen, which is visibly large and short with a prominent fat apron.
The video shows the initial views encountered after the hernia has been reduced from inside, revealing the internal anatomy and surgical field.
The surgeon performs meticulous adhesiolysis to clear the abdomen of adhesions, releasing 15 centimeters of dead ischemic small bowel from the incarcerated incisional hernia.
The surgeon identifies a tight five to six centimeter hernia defect in the midline and clears all surrounding adhesions, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the hernia repair.

The surgeon manipulates the small bowel, which is attached to a long loop of mesentery, placing it into the pelvis. A small suprapubic incision is made in the lower abdomen, through which the bowel is delivered out. A side-to-side resection anastomosis is performed, and the bowel is subsequently replaced back into the abdomen using an Alexis wound retractor.
It's very important to make sure that there's no twist on the mesentery or on the bowel as it can be easily missed whilst doing laparoscopic procedures.

The surgeon inspects the abdominal cavity, confirming the absence of fecal or purulent contamination. A biodesign mesh is prepared for repairing the hernia defect following the small bowel resection anastomosis.
Please see my video on various type of meshes that can be used intra-abdominally for laparoscopic repairs.
The surgeon attempts to parachute a mesh onto the abdominal wall defect using a single straight needle stitch, but the thickness of the abdominal wall prevents the needle from penetrating through.

The surgeon lifts the mesh onto the hernia defect and anchors it to the abdominal wall using protac tuckers. The mesh is positioned to achieve a four to five centimeter overlap with the hernia edge, although the superior edge lacks satisfactory coverage due to a large fat adhesion.
The surgeon removes the previously placed mesh and decides to use another biodesign mesh to achieve satisfactory coverage at the superior edge of the hernia defect.
Please remember that a biological mesh lacks the strength and stiffness of a composite or a synthetic mesh. Therefore, it's not always very easy to place the mesh against the abdominal wall.

All steps necessary should be taken to make sure that the mesh is properly placed for a satisfactory repair. In this particular patient because of the odd shape of the abdomen and the emergency repair further challenges were faced.
The surgeon successfully places the mesh against the abdominal wall, achieving satisfactory coverage around the hernia defect despite previous challenges with wall thickness and fat adhesion.

This patient had a return to theatre three days after the operation. This was because of the rise in CRP and CT showed a small bowel interloop collection which was successfully aspirated laparoscopically.
If you like my video, please hit the subscribe button. Thank you.
